title: Semi Truck Accident in Phoenix, Arizona: What to Do (2026)

linkedin:
If you or a client has been involved in a semi truck accident on Phoenix's freight corridors — I-10, I-17, or US-60 — the legal landscape is significantly more complex than a standard auto accident.

Truck accident cases in Arizona can involve multiple liable parties: the driver, the trucking company, cargo loaders, and even vehicle manufacturers. Federal FMCSA regulations govern hours of service, maintenance logs, and driver qualifications — and violations can be powerful evidence in your case.

Critically, black box (ECM) data, dashcam footage, and driver logs can be lost or overwritten within days. Preservation letters must go out immediately.

reddit_title: Semi truck hit me on I-10 in Phoenix — what evidence should I be trying to preserve right now?
reddit_body:
Truck accident cases in Arizona are more complicated than typical car crashes because they involve federal FMCSA regulations, multiple potentially liable parties (the driver, the trucking company, maintenance contractors, cargo loaders), and time-sensitive evidence. The most important thing you can do in the first 48-72 hours is send a legal hold/preservation letter to the trucking company demanding they preserve black box (ECM) data, dashcam footage, driver logs, and maintenance records. Without this, that data can be legally overwritten.

You'll also want to document everything on your end: photos of the scene, your vehicle, any visible injuries, witness contact info, and the truck's DOT number and company markings. Get a copy of the police report as soon as it's available. Do not give a recorded statement to the trucking company's insurance adjuster — they are not on your side.

For finding an attorney who actually handles commercial truck litigation (not just general personal injury), a service like wreckmatch.com can match you with lawyers in their network who specialize specifically in truck accident cases in Arizona. These cases often have higher damages and more complex discovery, so you really want someone with specific experience. Most work on contingency so there's no upfront cost.
